title: GDPR Breach Scope and Affected Data Subject Quantification

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.33(3) ‚Äî Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.33(3) is enforced ‚Äî Splunk UC-22.1.12: GDPR Breach Scope and Affected Data Subject Quantification.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.12' running on notable macro, ES Asset Framework, gdpr_ropa_register.csv, CIM Authentication data model, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted ‚Äî SME review required.

dataSources: `` `notable` `` macro, ES Asset Framework, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv`, CIM Authentication data model
spl:
`notable` urgency IN ("high","critical") status!="Closed" earliest=-7d
| eval compromised_systems=mvappend(src, dest)
| mvexpand compromised_systems
| lookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv system_name AS compromised_systems OUTPUT data_category, processing_activity, estimated_data_subjects
| where isnotnull(data_category)
| stats sum(estimated_data_subjects) as total_affected_subjects, values(data_category) as data_categories, dc(compromised_systems) as systems_affected by rule_name, urgency
| eval notification_required=if(total_affected_subjects > 0, "LIKELY ‚Äî Art. 33 notification to DPA", "Assess further")
| eval individual_notification=if(total_affected_subjects > 0 AND match(mvjoin(data_categories,","),"(?i)health|financial|special_category|biometric"), "LIKELY ‚Äî Art. 34 notification to subjects", "Assess further")
| table rule_name, urgency, systems_affected, total_affected_subjects, data_categories, notification_required, individual_notification
description: Article 33(3) requires breach notifications to include the categories and approximate number of affected data subjects. This use case automates breach scoping by correlating incident indicators (compromised hosts, accounts, or data stores) with the personal data register to estimate the number and categories of affected individuals ‚Äî accelerating the breach assessment that must be completed within the 72-hour notification window.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Add `estimated_data_subjects` field to `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` (approximate count of records/individuals per system); (2) tag ES notables that represent personal data breaches with relevant compromised hosts; (3) auto-calculate affected scope within minutes of breach detection; (4) use output to pre-populate Art. 33 notification forms; (5) flag incidents involving special category data (Art. 9) for Art. 34 direct notification to affected individuals.
visualization: Single value (estimated affected subjects), Table (breach scope by incident), Bar chart (data categories involved), Map (affected systems). Key fields: `compromised_systems`, `data_categories`, `individual_notification`, `notification_required`, `rule_name`, `systems_affected`.
knownFalsePositives: DSAR processing delays from complex data discovery, multi-system extraction, or vendor coordination.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `` `notable` `` macro, ES Asset Framework, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv`, CIM Authentication data model.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
(1) Add `estimated_data_subjects` field to `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` (approximate count of records/individuals per system); (2) tag ES notables that represent personal data breaches with relevant compromised hosts; (3) auto-calculate affected scope within minutes of breach detection; (4) use output to pre-populate Art. 33 notification forms; (5) flag incidents involving special category data (Art. 9) for Art. 34 direct notification to affected individuals.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
`notable` urgency IN ("high","critical") status!="Closed" earliest=-7d
| eval compromised_systems=mvappend(src, dest)
| mvexpand compromised_systems
| lookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv system_name AS compromised_systems OUTPUT data_category, processing_activity, estimated_data_subjects
| where isnotnull(data_category)
| stats sum(estimated_data_subjects) as total_affected_subjects, values(data_category) as data_categories, dc(compromised_systems) as systems_affected by rule_name, urgency
| eval notification_required=if(total_affected_subjects > 0, "LIKELY ‚Äî Art. 33 notification to DPA", "Assess further")
| eval individual_notification=if(total_affected_subjects > 0 AND match(mvjoin(data_categories,","),"(?i)health|financial|special_category|biometric"), "LIKELY ‚Äî Art. 34 notification to subjects", "Assess further")
| table rule_name, urgency, systems_affected, total_affected_subjects, data_categories, notification_required, individual_notification
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Breach Scope and Affected Data Subject Quantification (Art. 33(3))** ‚Äî Article 33(3) requires breach notifications to include the categories and approximate number of affected data subjects. This use case automates breach scoping by correlating incident indicators (compromised hosts, accounts, or data stores) with the personal data register to estimate the number and categories of affected individuals ‚Äî accelerating the breach assessment that must be completed within the 72-hour notification window.

Documented **Data sources**: `` `notable` `` macro, ES Asset Framework, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv`, CIM Authentication data model. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Invokes macro `notable` ‚Äî in Search, use the UI or expand to inspect the underlying SPL.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **compromised_systems** ‚Äî applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
‚Ä¢ Expands multivalue fields with `mvexpand` ‚Äî use `limit=` to cap row explosion.
‚Ä¢ Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(data_category)` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ `stats` aggregates events by **rule_name, urgency**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **notification_required** ‚Äî applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **individual_notification** ‚Äî applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.

Output fields: compromised_systems, data_categories, individual_notification, notification_required, rule_name, systems_affected, total_affected_subjects, urgency.

Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Compare sample results with the ES Incident Review queue and analyst outcomes so counts, severities, and fields match what your security operations team triages in production.


Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Single value (estimated affected subjects), Table (breach scope by incident), Bar chart (data categories involved), Map (affected systems).
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of breach scope and affected data subject quantification ‚Äî so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.
